items.title: Residents of mobile home park in Vernon, B.C., without power indefinitely
items.summary_auto: The site's electrical system was disconnected because it has been deemed hazardous by Technical Safety B.C. Crown Villa's landlord says she cannot afford to pay for the repairs.

items.title: Is Canada's 'Netflix tax' worth the hassle it's getting from the U.S.?
items.summary_auto: As U.S. lawmakers press the White House to examine whether Canada's Online Streaming Act is an unfair trade practice — and retaliate if necessary — CBC's The House speaks to experts about whether the bill is worth all the hassle and why it's generated so much controversy.

items.title: Study finds hidden lake network beneath Arctic glaciers as climate change accelerates
items.summary_auto: Researchers have uncovered the locations of 37 subglacial lakes in Canada’s Arctic, 35 of them discovered for the very first time — what could be a major scientific breakthrough in understanding the quickly-warming region.

items.title: For a party once thought endangered, the Liberals are enjoying a surprising afterlife
items.summary_auto: Supporters of the Liberal Party of Canada had much to celebrate in Montreal this weekend. For one thing, the Liberal Party continues to exist as a significant presence in Canadian politics. Fifteen years ago that seemed very far from guaranteed.

items.title: ‘She clearly wanted help,' patient who saw woman collapse before Ontario hospital death tells inquest
items.summary_auto: Heather Winterstein's skin was discoloured and she was struggling to control her body in a wheelchair the day she died at the St. Catharines, Ont., hospital, a woman who was waiting to see a doctor that day told a coroner’s inquest that began March 30.

items.title: Her daughter was killed by gunfire in Gaza school. For her and others, 6-month Israeli ceasefire isn't working
items.summary_auto: Tent schools like the one where nine-year-old Ritaj Rihan was killed by gunfire this week are symbolic of the fragile hope Palestinians have for the future and the paralyzing fear they still live with today. Six months after Israel and Hamas reached the first phase of a ceasefire that was supposed to end fighting in Gaza, humanitarian organizations have given it a failing grade.

items.title: Liberal members will vote today on whether they'd like to see age restrictions for social media
items.summary_auto: Liberal members will decide Saturday whether they think the government should restrict young Canadians' access to social media platforms and artificial intelligence chatbots, as the national convention winds down in Montreal.

items.title: IN PHOTOS | Inside an Orthodox Easter celebration in Canada
items.summary_auto: While most Canadians have packed away their Easter decorations, Ukrainian, Greek and other Orthodox communities in the country are gathering to celebrate Easter this weekend. Here's a look at Toronto's Ukrainian community and how members mark the religious holiday with a basket blessing.

items.title: Fact check: Deepfake Mark Carney promoting crypto scheme
items.summary_auto: No, Prime Minister Mark Carney is not promoting a get-rich-quick crypto scheme. But YouTube ads like this one are trying to scam you — and they use fake CBC articles to do it.

items.title: Top 3 takeaways from the Artemis II mission and what's next
items.summary_auto: While their trip looked like fun during the numerous news conferences and videos we saw, the four astronauts were hard at work with scientific observations, medical experiments and exercise. And they also had to deal with unexpected issues.

items.title: Other countries are looking to end animal testing. In Canada, there's a holdup
items.summary_auto: Other countries, like the U.K, the United States and the European Union have all dedicated funding and detailed roadmaps to replace animal testing in research settings. And while Canada has a strategy to replace animals used in chemical and toxicity testing, there is still no plan for those used in biomedical testing.

items.title: Why freshwater migratory fish need ‘safe passage’ globally as numbers decline
items.summary_auto: A global assessment on migratory freshwater fish species, published in March, found that there are 325 species of migratory fish crossing international borders that need deliberate action in order to survive.

items.title: Heather Winterstein 'looked terrified' before collapsing in St. Catharines hospital, witness tells inquest
items.summary_auto: Heather Winterstein's skin was discoloured and she was struggling to control her body in a wheelchair the day she died at the St. Catharines, Ont., hospital, a woman who was waiting to see a doctor that day told a coroner’s inquest that began March 30.

items.title: Farmers fight Liberal high-speed rail plan ahead of crucial byelections
items.summary_auto: With crucial federal byelections coming up on Monday, some farmers along the Liberal government's proposed route of a future high-speed rail line say whatever compensation they'd be offered wouldn't be worth the potential impacts on their lands and livelihoods.

items.title: Judge orders pause on signature validation process for Alberta independence petition
items.summary_auto: A judge has granted a month-long stay preventing Alberta’s chief electoral officer from certifying the results of a petition to force a referendum on a proposal for Alberta to separate from Canada.

items.title: 'It means so much': Highway of Tears' family members unveil Pillar of Hope in Prince George
items.summary_auto: Family members of Indigenous women and girls who have gone missing or been murdered along the Highway of Tears gathered in Prince George, B.C., to witness the unveiling of a monument called the Pillar of Hope.

items.title: Kamala Harris says she 'might' make another presidential run in 2028
items.summary_auto: ​Former U.S. vice-president Kamala Harris, who was the Democratic Party's nominee for the presidency in 2024, said on Friday that she was considering running for ‌president again in 2028.

items.title: Scientists want to build an outdoor lab in Ontario to study decomposing human bodies
items.summary_auto: A Windsor-based forensic scientist is working to bring an outdoor lab to Kingsville that would study how human bodies decompose over time.

items.title: More than 3 dozen new measles cases recorded in Manitoba in late March, early April
items.summary_auto: Manitoba recorded three dozen more confirmed and probable cases of measles over the final days of March and into early April.

items.title: U.S. appears to lower Canadian softwood lumber tariffs — but uncertainty remains
items.summary_auto: The U.S. Department of Commerce has posted its preliminary tariff determination for the sector, estimated at just short of 25 per cent, lower than the current duty rate of more than 35 per cent.

items.title: Sentebale, charity that Prince Harry co-founded, sues him for defamation
items.summary_auto: A charity co-founded by Prince Harry in Africa to honour his late mother, Princess Diana, has sued him for defamation after he stepped down as a patron last year.

items.title: Appeal court upholds stay of THC-impaired driving charge in death of 9-year-old Sask. girl
items.summary_auto: Taylor Kennedy was charged with impaired driving causing death after Baeleigh Maurice, 9, died when Kennedy hit her with her truck on Sept. 9, 2021. The Crown appealed a judge's decision to stay the charge over trial delays.

items.title: Deputy minister found breaching ethics rules says she was following diversity mandate
items.summary_auto: Deputy minister of national defence Christiane Fox says she was trying to bring in outside perspectives when she influenced her former department to hire an acquaintance.

items.title: NDP's Leah Gazan calls MMIWG2SLGBTQQIA+ critics 'bigots'
items.summary_auto: NDP MP Leah Gazan is standing by her use of the initialism MMIWG2SLGBTQQIA+ and denouncing those who are mocking her online for using the term.

items.title: Lockdown lifted at University of Ottawa, man arrested nearby
items.summary_auto: The Ottawa Police Service says it's arrested a man in connection with an incident at the University of Ottawa Friday afternoon that left the school under lockdown.

items.title: Police arrest suspect after Molotov cocktail thrown at home of OpenAI's Sam Altman
items.summary_auto: Officers arrested a 20-year-old man suspected of throwing a Molotov cocktail at OpenAI CEO Sam Altman's San Francisco home on Friday and then making threats at the company's headquarters, police and the company said.

items.title: U.S. allows nearly 4,500 South African refugees since October — and only 3 from elsewhere
items.summary_auto: President Donald Trump has made good on a promise to drastically cut the number of refugees into the U.S., with white South Africans being the top priority over those fleeing conflict, poverty and other threats to their safety from different countries.

items.title: First Nations leaders strongly oppose B.C. plan to pause DRIPA as Eby defends move ahead of confidence vote
items.summary_auto: First Nations leaders in B.C. are urging B.C. NDP MLAs to reject Premier David Eby’s plan to  suspend some sections of the province’s Declaration on the Rights of Indigenous Peoples Act (DRIPA), as the government prepares to bring the proposal forward as a confidence vote.

items.title: Shelter-in-place lifted after chlorine gas exposure hospitalized 8 at Victoria swimming pool
